item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bethesda called. They want their NPC back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Update made now that I've returned to the site and become somewhat active once again. A long belated "thanks" for the catch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor nitpick: Lockheed Martin isn't hyphenated. Also, the full name would be "Lockheed Martin Corporation", although you could go to "Lockheed Martin" after the first one without breaking anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I totally didn't get that he was a glitched out video game character until I came to the comments. Hmm. Either way, I still like and upvote this.

It appears we both have a taste for tragic reality benders contained through primarily psychological means, yes? Also… am I the only one that found Dr. "Gordon fucking Freeman" (don't feel bad, Dr Leonard, I also didn't pick up on it) kind of tragic? Here was this brilliant physicist who pierced the very veil of the universe… and now he's reduced to a wandering dementia patient, helpless despite all his glitchy glory.

That, and the comic creepiness of somebody "glitching out" in real life, with the implication that our reality is a simulation, makes this a pretty meaty and enjoyable entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"formerly theoretical physicist Dr. G█████ F██████"

Can't believe I didn't notice this was Gordon fucking Freeman

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: garrys mod life would suck, but its so enjoyable to read about

See, now if he had gotten a debug menu, then he could have done real damage. But just going straight for modifying memory values that you think you've figured out? He's lucky that he just glitched himself and didn't brick the whole universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the critique.

How would you recommend fixing that first sentence? I thought it was fine, but after re-reading it 100x you got me paranoid. I changed it to, "is a 37 year-old Caucasian male humanoid," which uses "male" as an adjective instead of a noun. I decided to keep "humanoid," as his anomalous effects are so pronounced that even calling him human would be more of a presumption than a statement of fact.

Overall I just can't decide if I like this. The concept is great, as is a lot of the detail work, but… Hm. I'll have to think about this and return to it.

What else don't you like about it? I'm always open to specific critique, especially from experienced members.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I almost always refer to them as humanoids and not humans; I generally view the Foundation as never making that assumption.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1924 is a 37 year-old humanoid Caucasian male, formerly theoretical physicist Dr. G█████ F██████ working at Lockheed-Martin's █████ ████ Research Facility.

This sentence kept tripping me up.

  • Humanoid? No, he's human, albeit subject to anomalous effects. Humanoid means resembling a human. I guess you could make an argument that it's the Foundation's perspective, but I find that 'meh'.
  • Caucasian isn't scientific, though you can get away with that.
  • A male. Since you've specified humanoid, now I have to wonder: male what? Presumably male human but that is really starting to sound silly.

Also the lobotomy suggestion sounds like you're trying too hard.

Overall I just can't decide if I like this. The concept is great, as is a lot of the detail work, but… Hm. I'll have to think about this and return to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, thanks for the feedback, but I'm sorry you feel that way.

I did not intend this to be a crossover at all, just a subtle nod to my favorite video game character. He could just as easily be a Gerald Frazier or a Grover Feldman or even George Foreman.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with this. Feels a bit silly, breaks the mood for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. To be frank, the Gordon Freeman/Black Mesa reference was a bit too mood-breaking for me. It's just so obvious (for gamers) that it feels more like an unwelcome crossover than a shout-out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Abettik, I gotta say, I really like this. It took me til about halfway through the incident log for my suspicions to be confirmed, but man was it worth it. The image of the guy spazzing in the floor though XD. I did feel the final note was, well, a hammer to drive the concept-nail soundly home, but hey, you gotta do what you gotta do. If people don't quite get it and this was the best way, more power to you. Honestly, that was a small enough concern not to affect my voting in any way.

+1'd with gusto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also noticed G||||| F|||||| was our dear doctor Freeman.
I wish I could upvote twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As my first skip, I was resolved to be happy with some constructive criticism and a single digit positive score. But instead, wow. Thanks for all the support and praise! This is a great, friendly community and its inspired me to write a new skip that I may submit for 2000.

Since its been almost 48 hours, I've decided to share the Easter Eggs I included:

+ Easter Eggs
- Hide Eggs

Theoretical physicist Dr. G█████ F██████ is theoretical physicist Dr. Gordon Freeman, from the Half-Life video game series.

█████ ████ Research Facility is Black Mesa Research Facility, the fictional location of the first Half-Life.

The incident dates are all release dates from various Half-Life games and expansions.

Personal Note:

J█████ is my brother Joseph who recently (naturally) conceived a baby with his wife, against very low odds. He proudly sent an ultrasound around to the family, and I made the comment that it looks like a little Han Solo in Carbonite. You can judge that for yourself: http://i302.photobucket.com/albums/nn113/pfrangip/Real%20Life/carbonite_zps21bcc3d8.png

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was actually inspired by a real correspondence for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -kicks Mr. Carbon-

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed it, and picked up on the inspiration right away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most "breathing programming" is just a bar that appears when you go underwater anyway. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final note is what makes it for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Abettik, I salute you. This is my favorite firstie in ages. Have an upvgggggggggg%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%% Abettik, I salute you. This is my favorite firstie in ages. Have an upvgggggggggg%%%%…..

(etc.)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…All I have to say is- I immediately thought of Garry's Mod.
Yes. Yes good. You have done a service to us all.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. Also be sure and check out their pals, Freddiew and co: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YbxRp6Dg_VQ

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! And, yes, as I mentioned earlier, Garry's Mod was my inspiration for that particular event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, notice the site is in Arizona. This is assuming its probably desolate desert Arizona. A guy jumping straight up and reaching the stratosphere wouldn't be noticed by many in the middle of the desert and a passing plane would be rare. Even if some high-on-peyote desert dweller saw him, the Foundation has certainly dealt with far more extensive breaches.

As for the cold, breathing, radiation, whatever, the guy has a leg for a head and we already know he doesn't need food or water or sleep. I would imagine that one of the more boring unlisted documents would be an experiment log to see what exactly can hurt him, which is to say, not much. He probably added iddqd to the source code somewhere before hitting :wq

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit: Disregard that. Didn't realize the .31 m jumps were cumulative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, a human-sized target over 20 km away can't be resolved with the naked eye…

I could almost buy this, if not for the small detail that he went through the intervening space. The only concession I can make on this point is that we don't know how far this facility is from signs of civilization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
anterograde amnesia

I'd upvote this just for using that term correctly.

A maintenance worker found SCP-1924 in the corner of a Site ██ conference room, upside down with its head sunken into the floor. Its body was suspended in the air, with its arms and legs flailing wildly in an erratic, elastic fashion. While Foundation personnel were deciding what to do, SCP-1924 stood up normally, smiled, and asked if there was a problem. It then returned to its cell upon request.

Oh god my sides

Edit: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EWVES3v0v6I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
leg-headed guy?

Breathing issues?

It's amusing to me that you're worried about his ability to breathe 20 km above earth's surface, but not because he has a leg for a head. That suspension of disbelief can be a weird thing sometimes…

Also, a human-sized target over 20 km away can't be resolved with the naked eye (2 km seems to be the limit for seeing a human as even a dot), and the chances of a telescope catching it during the brief window he would be up there are… well, 'minute(As in "my newt is minute")' is not too strong a word. Anyone who did see it would probably blink it away like "That can't have just happened, I need some damn coffee".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As amusing as the last example is, the guy jumped over 20 km straight up with no repercussions? Nobody saw this floating leg-headed guy? Breathing issues?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not an exceptionally hard grader (badum-tish) — I have something like an 80/20 upvote/downvote split.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8Q2P4LjuVA8

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Thanks. Glad you liked it. I tried to walk that subtle line between too much giveaway and not enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. Glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woo, Scantron liked it too. That's another check for the bucket list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha. Though I did have three stillborn ideas that never made it past critique forum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for that! Last night my wife and I were cracking up watching that video.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Faux?

Really liked it.

I cringed a little bit at the this-is-what-is-the-thing note at the end, but not enough to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I approve of the noncompatabilityness.

That's a new word starting now.

Also secret video footage of the scip in action oh mah god containment breaaaaach http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AemxS2Xtbo8

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done! I was thinking the guy is a glitch in a video game the entire time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not even a video game person, and I still love IRL glitchiness. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, it's your first? Sorry have to downvote it, or you'll get full of yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback. I originally had the words in the addendum "code for a computer program" blacked out, but too many people were still clueless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback!

(Dr. Bright liked my first skip! My life is complete. )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! I appreciate the critique. In this case, though, I think I am going to keep the word "phony." I like the word and I think it fits what I am trying to get across. If you have a better synonym in mind, I'm all ears.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the critique! I made several of the changes you suggested. I couldn't really think of a better way to phrase "straight up in the air," though, and I would appreciate suggestions. Perhaps adding the word "hovering?"

Anyways, I'm sorry this wasn't up your alley, but I'm glad you found it neat enough to help out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A living glitch, like folks are saying; I like it. I think the last addendum makes it a little too neat, but given your spoiler above, it makes sense to have it there. Very nice article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A glitchy video game character in real life. i like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Phony" doesn't really seem clinical enough. Apart from that, I liked it, but didn't really /get/ it until I read your comments. But I didn't read that last addendum that thoroughly. I gave it a +1, because evn without really getting it it was a good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a few issues.

SCP-1924 exhibits extreme cognitive dysfunction: it does not demonstrate understanding that it is contained, it does not notice anything unusual during spatial distortions, and it suffers from extreme anterograde amnesia, being unable to recall most events beyond a two-hour time frame.

Firstly, the colon I feel would functional better as a semi. Secondly, this is a lot of information packed into one sentence. I feel that it would be less convoluted when the mention of amnesia be in a second sentence.

Its body was suspended in the air, with its arms and legs flailing wildly in an erratic, elastic fashion.

This is a bit vague and I'm unsure what exactly is going on here. Clarity would help.

SCP-1924 then jumped straight up in the air .31 meters

I think this phrase needs some work.

Overall it's pretty neat. Nothing up my alley though, so I can't upvote with any real interest, but I'll admit it's neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! You know what they say about imitation and flattery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough. I appreciate the feedback!

+ Answers
- Hide

As for your questions: he's suspended so that he doesn't teleport into lower or upper levels. He (usually) only teleports 39m or less, so the 50m is a buffer to keep him from teleporting into a lab or some other containment cell. It works most of the time. As for the delusion of him being employed, his mind is so fragmented that it doesn't matter what we tell him, he's not going to get anything out of it. That's not why we do it. The point is, we found an infinite loop that keeps him more or less stable and he _usually_ doesn't break during it. Think about playing a glitchy game or using a broken computer where you know it freezes and crashes if you do anything but this one set of events. You log into windows, immediately kill background process running, turn on Microsoft word, and if you turn the printer off and on again right after hitting print, you can get it to print. Anything else and it bluescreens… oh, and reboot every two hours, obviously, otherwise it crashes.

And to be honest, I thought the final note was a bit heavy handed myself. However, several people in forums (and even here, still) didn't understand the "glitch in reality" hook and I found myself forced to make it more explicit. The way I see it, if you're big into Video Games, you're going to understand what's going on pretty quickly. You probably got it from the description, and confirmed it from the first incident report. However, not everyone are gamers, or even computer people, and the visual imagery of "glitching" simply wasn't something familiar to them.

As tired as it seems since the Matrix, to my knowledge there's very few "reality is an illusion" or "reality is a computer program" skips. There is 1165, which I liked a lot and drew inspiration from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand a few parts of the containment procedures—what's the point of suspending the enclosure? Why do we bother keeping the SCP under the delusion that it is still employed by Lockheed Martin? As for the content itself, the take on something that's glitched out of reality is just…eh, it's been done enough in mainstream media to not be especially original or interesting to me. And without the glitch (which is basically hammered into you with that last note), it's just a bunch of weird stuff happening to some dude.

So, for me this just barely crosses the threshold from neutral into dv territory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Feel free to fix any typos/grammar mistakes you find.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. Hehe, I steal your post format.

Its a nice idea, and I think the concept and the logs carry this enough to be an amusing (if pity-evoking) read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reality is a computer program, and this guy is bugged. I like it.

There's still a few typos and its/it's confusion to look out for, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! I had read that skip before, and I liked him, but he wasn't the inspiration for this, nor did I even consider him when writing it. You can see my spoiler in a post below for what I was going for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I imagined they got the jumping event on camera, or just his total height and then divided.

I thought about the horror angle for a long time. There is even an attempted interview log on my sandbox. I felt he was coming across too… regular, however, and if I had gone full random, it would have been too silly. ("How many fingers am I holding up?" "OCTAGONS ARE STERILE. I LIKE MOTHS.")

If this fails, I may try again with more focus on seeing the horror of things from his side.
